# KV Cache Sharing in Homogeneous Multi-Agent Workflows

A key efficiency property of homogeneous multi-agent workflows—where all agents share the same base LLM—is that substantial contextual overlap exists between agent interactions. Shared task descriptions, intermediate reasoning steps, and common knowledge appear repeatedly across agents' inputs. When each agent is instantiated as a separate process, every instance must independently encode these overlapping prefixes, resulting in redundant computation that scales with the number of agents multiplied by the shared prefix length.

When a single LLM instance simulates multiple agents through multi-turn conversation—sequentially adopting each agent's system prompt while maintaining a unified conversation history—it maintains one continuous KV cache that grows incrementally. The prefill cost at each simulated agent turn scales only with the new tokens appended since the last turn, rather than the full accumulated prefix. Formally, if separate agent instances must each re-encode overlapping prefixes, the total computation scales with the sum of all prefix lengths across agents. The single-LLM simulator's cost scales with the total unique prefix length plus incremental additions, which is asymptotically no worse and typically substantially cheaper when agents share significant context.

This cache-sharing advantage is one of the mechanisms underlying the empirical finding that homogeneous multi-agent workflows can be faithfully and efficiently simulated by a single agent conducting multi-turn conversations. The simulation is not merely behaviorally equivalent but computationally cheaper, because the dominant cost in transformer inference—recomputing attention over long prefixes—is amortized across all simulated agent turns.

A fundamental limitation is that KV caches cannot be shared across different base models. In heterogeneous multi-agent workflows, where different agents use different LLMs, each model must maintain its own separate cache. No amount of contextual overlap between agents can be exploited for computational savings when the underlying model architectures or weights differ. This creates an inherent efficiency asymmetry between homogeneous and heterogeneous system designs: homogeneous systems can always be collapsed into a single-agent loop with shared caching, while heterogeneous systems must bear the full cost of maintaining parallel inference states. This asymmetry is one reason why naive heterogeneous composition often fails to outperform well-optimized homogeneous workflows despite the theoretical appeal of leveraging complementary model strengths.

## See Also

- [[z20260318-034|Homogeneous vs. Heterogeneous Multi-Agent Workflow Design]] — grounds the distinction between homogeneous and heterogeneous workflows and the single-agent simulation equivalence that KV cache sharing enables
- [[z20260318-001|Multi-Agent Architectures for Workflow Automation]] — contrasts the static model-to-role mapping in current frameworks with the efficiency implications of cache sharing
- [[Transformer KV Cache Optimization]] — elaborates the underlying KV cache mechanisms and memory management strategies that make cache sharing possible
